edibility: Too woody to eat; traditionally used as medicinal tea or extract for immune support and anti-inflammatory benefits. Historically valued as punkwood for carrying fire, soaking up liquids, and starting tinder.
taste: Bitter and resinous with subtle lemony and piney notes when brewed as tea or decoction.
search: Year-round perennial conk found on conifers (pine, fir, spruce) and dead/dying softwood snags. Distinctive reddish-orange to orange-yellow upper surface with concentric bands and black-brown lower surface. Key distinction from similar shelf fungi: characteristic color banding and hardwood-like density.
